Causes of Blocked Drains in Sydney Blocked drains are often caused by everyday materials that make their way into your pipes and gradually build up over time. Common causes include: Hair and soap scum : A leading cause of blockages in bathroom drains, hair and soap scum form stubborn clogs that restrict water flow. Grease and fats : Pouring cooking grease or fat down your kitchen sink may seem harmless, but it hardens as it cools, creating blockages.

Here’s what to look out for: Slow draining : If water in your sinks, bathtubs, or showers is draining slowly, it’s a clear indication that something is blocking the pipes. Gurgling noises : Strange noises from your drains mean there’s air trapped inside, often caused by a blockage further down. Foul odours : Unpleasant smells emanating from drains are typically a sign of decomposing materials like food or hair that are stuck inside your pipes.

Overflowing toilets : When flushing causes water to rise higher than normal, or even overflow, it suggests there’s a blockage deeper in the drain system. Water backing up : If water is backing up into your sinks or showers when you use appliances like the washing machine or dishwasher, it’s a sure sign of a blocked drain. If you notice any of these symptoms, it’s time to call a blocked drain plumber to unblock the drain before the problem escalates.

Foreign objects : Items like sanitary products, wipes, or small toys can cause serious blockages, especially in toilets. Tree roots : In older homes, tree roots can grow into pipes, causing significant damage and blockages in the sewer line. Debris in stormwater drains : Leaves, dirt, and other debris can clog outdoor stormwater drains, leading to water backups during heavy rain.

In NSW, Owner-builder work includes managing and doing construction or changes to your home that cost more than $10,000. Projects that fall into this category require council permission and must meet specific guidelines. Trust that we’ll guide you to ensure compliance with these. Who needs an owner-builder permit.

You need an owner-builder permit if you plan to handle building tasks worth over $10,000 yourself and won't be hiring a licensed builder. What is my job as an owner-builder. You must oversee the construction work, order materials, and manage the building site. This means making sure that all legal and safety rules are followed. You’re also in charge of getting all necessary approvals from the council or other authorities. What approvals do I need to start building.

Before starting the build, you need to have: Development consent or a Complying Development Certificate (CDC) Construction certificate from the council or a certified inspector Owner-builder permit to start the actual building
